company.name: Broadcom

marketCap.rationale: Computed from Avago's $15.00 IPO price and 235,888,203 shares outstanding immediately after the offering in the final prospectus.

company.description: Semiconductor and infrastructure software consolidator with critical exposure to networking and virtualization.

overview.title: Where chips meet enterprise rent extraction
paragraphs: Broadcom is an awkwardly elegant example of two different moat types living under one roof: hard-to-replicate semiconductor supply chains and highly monetizable enterprise software lock-in.
That split makes Broadcom less decentralizable than software vendors but more vulnerable than a pure semiconductor titan in specific categories like VMware-era virtualization.

moatNarrative: Broadcom's moat comes from custom silicon relationships, networking relevance, and a willingness to monetize mission-critical software with all the tenderness of a spreadsheet.
Customers may dislike the pricing; they also tend to dislike downtime, which is usually what preserves the margin.
decentralizationNarrative: The software side is replaceable in pockets. The semiconductor side is much less so.
That makes Broadcom a mixed case: some value capture looks structurally durable, while some looks like a generous invitation for Proxmox and friends.
company.snapshotNote: Curated early-2026 public-source snapshot. Exact ordering can drift with the tape.

moat.rationale: Broadcom combines hard-to-replicate hardware supply with entrenched enterprise software footprints.

decentralizability.rationale: Virtualization can be displaced more readily than Broadcom's semiconductor relevance.

profitability.rationale: The company has both pricing power and critical infrastructure exposure.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate valuation snapshot reflecting AI and infrastructure enthusiasm.

marketCap.rationale: Approximate market cap snapshot from public market trackers.

products.name: VMware virtualization stack
products.summary: Virtualization and private cloud control plane software.
products.whyItMatters: This is one of Broadcom's most obviously replaceable rent-heavy layers.
replacementSketch: Open virtualization stacks already exist and are increasingly production-worthy.
Incumbent pricing pressure often does half the adoption work for the alternatives.

alternatives.name: Proxmox VE
alternatives.summary: Open-source virtualization stack with increasing enterprise appeal.

readiness.rationale: Now credible for a wide range of production workloads.

costLeverage.rationale: Directly compresses premium VMware pricing logic.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Workload Portability Guild
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of migration, orchestration, and support operators that makes leaving proprietary virtualization less operationally terrifying.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Disrupt VMware lock-in by turning migration, support, and workload portability into competitive services rather than a vendor tax.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization angle is open workload portability and interoperable migration services instead of a single vendor support funnel.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Operators sell migration packages, managed support, and portability guarantees atop open virtualization stacks.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Signed migration reports, workload rollback checkpoints, and escrowed success milestones reduce fake migration claims.
failureModes: Enterprises fear downtime more than license bills
Support quality must be consistently high
adoptionPath: Scale through repeatable migration playbooks and audited support guarantees

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept meaningfully shifts control away from a single incumbent operator.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The participant and incentive model is plausible but still operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Current tools and market structure could support an initial version without waiting for a full paradigm shift.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If adopted, the concept would chip away at pricing power or default distribution leverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Proof-of-Availability Capacity Market
disruptionConcepts.summary: Compute operators sell verified spare capacity through open contracts that price uptime, migration readiness, and failure penalties instead of relying on one vendor's license moat.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Unlike the first concept's portability guild, this one turns enterprise capacity itself into a metered commodity market.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Lightning-style settlement and open virtualization stacks make it easier to pay only for delivered availability.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Operators expose cluster offers with explicit SLAs, and buyers route workloads toward the best price and redundancy mix.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Continuous health attestations, failover drills, and payout penalties tie revenue to real delivered availability.
failureModes: Enterprise buyers may still prefer single-vendor accountability
Attestation and billing complexity can overwhelm the price advantage
adoptionPath: Start with burst capacity and disaster-recovery workloads
Expand after migration and uptime proofs become routine

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept decentralizes enterprise capacity and uptime commitments across many operators rather than one licensed platform vendor.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination loop is credible because availability can be sold as a discrete service with explicit failover and penalty terms.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Most primitives already exist; the platform pieces are available, though attestation and buyer trust are still heavy lifts.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If it scales, it pressures VMware licensing power and Broadcom's ability to reprice existing virtualization estates.

products.name: Broadcom networking silicon
products.summary: Critical chips and connectivity layers for data-center and telecom infrastructure.
products.whyItMatters: This is the part of Broadcom that remains stubbornly physical, capital-intensive, and supply-chain bound.
replacementSketch: Open networking software matters, but replacing the silicon layer itself is much slower and more capital-intensive.
Localized electronics tooling may erode some hardware rents over time, but not overnight and not at frontier scale.

alternatives.name: Open networking software stacks
alternatives.summary: Open switching and control plane efforts that erode some software-side networking lock-in.

openness.rationale: Moves some value away from closed control surfaces.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Helpful, but the silicon supply chain remains stubbornly concentrated.

readiness.rationale: Useful in pockets, still far from replacing the entire incumbent stack.

costLeverage.rationale: Reduces some premium capture without overturning the hardware layer.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Switch Design Commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: A white-box networking ecosystem with open switch designs, firmware, and integration services that erodes dependence on proprietary silicon bundles.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Attack proprietary networking margins by opening the design and integration layer around white-box hardware.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The disruptive angle is open hardware and shared design knowledge, because silicon economics matter more here than Bitcoin-native settlement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Hardware designers, firmware teams, and integrators coordinate around open switch references and shared certification suites.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Interoperability labs, open test fixtures, and published throughput benchmarks reduce fake performance claims.
failureModes: Large buyers still prefer proven integrated support
adoptionPath: Start in labs, edge deployments, and cost-sensitive data centers
Expand with stronger certification and support ecosystems

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept meaningfully shifts control away from a single incumbent operator.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The participant and incentive model is plausible but still operationally demanding.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Current tools and market structure could support an initial version without waiting for a full paradigm shift.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If adopted, the concept would chip away at pricing power or default distribution leverage.

disruptionConcepts.name: Refurbished Network Appliance Microfactory
disruptionConcepts.summary: Local shops refurbish, re-case, and modularize switch and network appliance hardware so buyers can keep more infrastructure in service instead of rebuying closed vendor boxes on vendor timelines.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Unlike the first concept's open new-design push, this one attacks replacement cycles through refurbishment and modular reuse.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Open hardware interfaces and local recovery loops keep value in reused components instead of mandatory full-box replacement.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Buyers source refurbished chassis, controllers, and spares from competing local or regional shops with published specs.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Port tests, burn-in logs, and component provenance records make refurbished gear comparable enough to trust.
failureModes: Warranty expectations and support burdens can push buyers back to incumbents
High-end networking reliability demands may exceed what small shops can guarantee
adoptionPath: Begin with lab, edge, and secondary network environments
Move upward only where modular test coverage earns trust

decentralizationFit.rationale: This concept decentralizes network appliance life-cycle work into refurbishment and local modular assembly.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: The coordination loop is credible because burn-in data and modular specs let buyers compare refurbished hardware from many shops.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The enabling pieces exist, but reuse loops are real, but enterprise-grade reliability and support still constrain the addressable share.

incumbentPressure.rationale: If it scales, it pressures Broadcom's replacement-cycle leverage and proprietary infrastructure packaging.
